speaker: interviewee
text: It's marvelous equipment, but it still needs to get up to speed. In Demblin--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: --how long were you there altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Demblin we were from 1942-- '42 to '44. Then in 1944 the Germans were realizing that the Russians are coming closer and closer, and they decided to transfer us to a different camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now but as a child in Demblin, you're 8, 9, 10 years old. What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp? I didn't do very much. I stayed pretty much hidden in the barracks most of the time because the women, the older people, went out to work so I really tried to avoid any sight. But, I mean, the Germans knew that there were children in the camp, so I just stayed in the barracks, and I played-- if there were children in my barrack, I would play with the children, sort of keep the kids in order. But I tried to avoid going out as much as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you play with? Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, come to think of it now, there was nothing to play with. Probably just imaginary things that children usually make up their own imaginary things. I-- off-hand, I can't remember what I played with. But I do remember one story very vividly. My sisters brought in some potatoes, and there was a kitchen there that we could go and cook, you know, the food, if you had a potato, whatever. And my sister once gave me this potato, two potatoes, to go to cook for her. And all I had to do was take the potatoes, put a little bit of water in, and just boil the potatoes so they were soft. But there were some women in the kitchen at that time talked me out of it, and they took away the potato. I gave it to them. They said they want it. They were hungry, and I gave them the potatoes. And that night my sister came back from working in the field all day, and she was very, very angry because she had risked her life in order to bring in the potatoes so we could eat, and I gave it away. And I remember my sister gave me such a beating.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I reminded this my sister many, many years ago-- as a matter of fact, because she does not remember doing that to me at all-- and I said, don't you remember? You hit me so hard, and I cried because I gave away the potatoes. And she said, but why did you do it? I said, well, they told me they were hungry, and they wanted the potatoes. So that story has always stuck in my mind too. I remember it, you know? It's things like this that you, you can't forget. This was Demblin.

speaker: interviewee
text: But life was pretty much routine. You know, you'd get up in the morning. They called you for an appel to count your numbers. They called you by your number. We all had numbers. On our necks, wore metal pieces of numbers. And you were lucky that day if they did not select another group to take out someplace else. The women went to work. The men went to work. You came home. You get the piece of bread and the little, dirty boil of soup, and that was life for two years. There was nothing else to do, plus the dirt and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1944, like I said, when the-- when the Germ-- the Russians were coming closer and the Germans were realizing that the war is getting closer to an end, they transferred us to Czestochowa, also in Poland. They took us by train, and this is where I remember the German Shepherds. The SS came, and they took us out. We were on the train for overnight. I remember it was night time, and we finally arrived there in the morning, the following morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of train were you on?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a boxcars, close, where they take cattle. And I remember one day that the train had to stop probably to change probably men, you know, the workers, and they did open the doors. They opened the boxcar, and it was a beautiful-- it was in the summer time too-- it was a gorgeous day. The sun was shining. The fields were beautifully green, and the Polish people were looking at us at the train and making faces and yelling dirty names at us. Nobody came to our rescue, nobody. Instead of them giving us water or giving us food, they were standing and mocking us and yelling names at us. And I remember the SS standing with the German Shepherds, with their dogs, and then they finally closed the doors up again. And we arrived to Czestochowa, and they separated the men and the women and the 11 children that were finally left. And I was there among the children. I was the oldest. At that time I think I was already 11-- yeah, 11 years old. And I was holding a six-month-old little baby that had-- that actually was born in the camp in Demblin. And the German looked at me, and he told me in German [GERMAN], throw the child away. And, of course, I didn't want to throw the child away, so what I did I put the child down. And I noticed that all the women were, were being disrobed. They had to throw away the old uniforms. And the men, I didn't see. The men were completely taken away in another section of the camp. And when the German told me to throw the child down, he also told me to run. I don't know what made this German soldier to tell me to run. I don't know. For that split second maybe I reminded him either of his own child. Maybe I reminded him of a grandchild because I was a blond little girl. I don't know what made him do those things, but he told me to run, and I ran into the pile of the dirty clothes where the women had just taken off all their clothing. And as I was laying in the dirty pile of clothes, the same German soldier that had a little bit of pity for a split second, a little bit of humanity in him for that split second, took out a gun and shot all the 11 children right in front of me while I was laying in, in that dirty pile of clothes. There was an open pit and he just with a machine gun went boom, boom, boom, boom like this and he-- that was the end of all the kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then finally when it came dark I ran into the barrack where the women were going in, and as I came into the barrack all the women started attacking me, asking me all sorts of questions. Where are the children? Where-- what happened to the children? What happened to the children? And, of course, my sisters were just overwhelmed with happiness and tears when they saw me because they too thought that I was going to be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, I don't know. Somehow or another I had enough sense not to say anything. I just said I don't know what happened to those children. I just don't know. And eventually, of course, all the mothers learned the fate of the children because the children never came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the first night when we arrived at Czestochowa. And then the following day we had to get up again for the appel and counting and giving us new numbers. And they had an ammunition factory, a bullet factory, and all the women and the men worked in the bullet factory, but not together.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked with my sisters. What I used to do-- and at that time I already had to go out to work. I was already older, and I was picking up the bullets that fell out of the machines. As they were making the bullets, sometimes a bullet that was not a perfect bullet would fall out, and I had to gather up all those bullets on the machine. And one day I remember while I was being in the factory, my sister, Anne, my sister Hannah, older sister, her machine got stuck. The bullets got stuck in one of the machines. And the kapo, the woman that was there, the kapo, took my sister aside and gave her 20 lashes with a-- with a, a leather strap right in front of everybody because her machine got stuck. Not that she could help it. It wasn't her fault, but she punished her right away because her machine was not working. And finally they were able to fix the machine and, of course, the bullets started coming. And this was my-- pretty much for a whole year, we did that. We worked in the-- in the factory making bullets. And life in there too was very, very bad. Again, the sanitation, the hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: The morale had already at that time was already beginning to get very, very bad because people-- it was already dragging out for four years. It didn't seem like the end is ever going to come, and people were already giving up. A lot of people actually gave up, and the ones that gave up so easily died too. They just said, what's the sense in living? There's no-- there's no sense in living anymore. Reva, how much time did you spend in Czestochowa being afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czestochowa? Well, the whole year while I was there, because even though I worked in the factory my life still was very much in danger because I still was a little girl, tried to pretend that I was an adult, tried to pretend that I could work, you know? But my life was in danger every moment. I mean, any German could have just shot me, you know? Nothing would have prevented him to kill me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I guess-- you know what? I've always had a belief that my destiny was to survive. I honestly feel that there was a reason for me to survive. And when you walk around and you're afraid of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Living in fear?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you never know. You're always constantly looking over your shoulder because you never know is this going to be your last moment? Are you going to die tomorrow? You know, you're just never, never know. You're a constantly fear that maybe any word I say or anything, any action that I do, this soldier might kill me, because they had absolutely no-- what is the word? They, themselves, there was no morality there. I mean, this man, this soldier-- the one that shot the 11 children-- I don't know. He had no conscience, so what-- he could have just as well have killed me too. It's a terrible fear. It's a fear that, that nags at you constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things change in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, January 17, 1945 things began to change. We had heard that the war was coming to an end, the Russians were coming closer and closer. And actually started January 16th, 1945, and there was a lot of rumor going around in the camp. A lot of whispering going around that the Germans, they're losing the war and that the Russians are coming in and we're going to be liberated and things like that. But of January 16th of that evening, the Germans knew that the end was coming already. The Russians were pretty close to Czestochowa. They decided to take the people out of the camps. They came back at night. They took everybody out, and some, they left in camp. They called out all the people for an appel in the morning, all the women and men, and they called you by numbers. Now, when my number came up, my oldest sister Hannah walked out. When they called Reva Mandelbaum, my sister walked out because she knew that if I would have gone out, I would have been killed that evening too. They were taking all the women and older men and they were putting them on trains. They were going to take them all to Buchenwald, to another camp, because the Germans were seeing that they were losing, and they didn't want the Russians to find anybody in the camps. They were trying to clean out the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that night they took my sister, Hannah, instead of me, my sister, Eda, and my brother all out into, to the box cars. My sister Hannah at that time was already 16 years old, and she knew that if I were to go, that they would not-- I would have never survived. They would have killed me. So when they called my name, she walked out, knowing full well that this might be-- she might never return, that she might be killed. So I was left in the camp with my sister Mindall. They didn't take everybody. They just left a few in the camp. And we were sitting the whole day while my-- obviously, other people were waiting on the boxcars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meanwhile, the bombing started. The Russians were bombarding the city Czestochowa. And they knew it because there were five camps in that city. And Czestochowa too was also very famous for the Black Madonna. It was a very Catholic-- all Poland was Catholic, of course. 95% were Catholics. But Czestochowa had the Black Madonna, and so we were in this camp, and they had five different slave labor camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that night the bombs were starting to fall. They were bombing the camp and the city all together. And I was sitting in the barracks with my sister Mindall. They had already taken away my sister Eda, my sister Hannah, and my brother Mark, Moishe. How many were left in the barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five. There were just five of us. My sister Mindall, my sister, Eda, Hannah, and Moishe, my brother Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you and Mindall were among how many?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were just a few hundred left in the camp. The rest were all taken out to the-- to the barracks-- to the train. And we were sitting and waiting, and they started bombing the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is really a cute little story. While my sister Mindall and I were sitting and they were bombing the camp, the Germans were already running away because they knew what was coming. My sister Mindall said to me, Reva, you are a little girl. Why don't you go out to the kitchen and see if we can find any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being a 12-year-old little girl at that time, I didn't pay much attention. I said, OK, I'll run into the kitchen, because the Germans were already leaving. It was such complete chaos in the camp. And I went into the kitchen, and unfortunately I did not find any food. There was no bread or anything left at all. But, I mean, I could have been killed because the bombs were falling. They were bombing the city. And my sister Mindall sends me out to look for food. So I ran out to the kitchen and, of course, I came back, there was no food, there was nothing left. And we stayed that whole night in the camp while the Germans were bomb-- the Russians actually were bombarding the camps and the city. And we were waiting and waiting and waiting. And then finally on January 17th of 1945 in the morning, we found that the people were not taken away. They were caught on the train because the Germans-- the Russians were too close so they could not be transported anymore, and then they let the people out. So all the people that were taken away that evening came back into the camp, but, of course, at that point we were already free because we were liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's not the end of the story either, because the problem was when we were liberated, everybody was running in different directions. You know what it was like? Like you let animals out of a cage. You, you became so wild you really did not know where to go or what to do. It was sheer chaos. Nobody knew which direction to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: As it happened, my sister and my brother ran away. They separated from us. They were going in one direction, but we all had one goal. We all said to each other, when we've survived-- when we survive, we all going to meet in the town where my mother and father were born in Demblin, the first camp that we were at, because that was our parents' hometown, and a lot of my mother's family lived there. And we thought that if anybody at all had survived, that this is the place that they would go. So we all decided this is the place to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister, my brother split. They went in one direction. We went in another direction, and it was complete chaos in the camp, complete chaos. Was it frightening? Were you frightened of the Russians or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was fright-- well, we did not really know the Russians because, you know, the Russians themselves were frightened because they too were very, very poor. It was a poor army. They had nothing to give us either. And what happened when they came into our camp, they were very, very angry with us because we made bullets. You know, we worked in ammunition factories make bullets to kill them. So they were very, very angry, you know, till they realized, of course, that we were prisoners. We were doing this not of our own free will. We had to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was sheer chaos in that camp, sheer chaos. People did not know which direction to run-- to run. It's like animals in a cage. You know when you open up a cage and you let animals out after they've been in a cage for a long, long time, they don't know what to do. They don't know which way to go. And people started running in each and every direction. Who did you go with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always was with my older sister Hannah, my older sister. She always watched over me. That's the sister that also went out when they called my name. She was the one. And had, had the Germans succeeded that night to take them all to Buchenwald, to that camp, she probably would have been killed because the war still went on. I was liberated January 17, 1945. The war did not end till May of '45. So had they-- had the Germans succeeded in taking that transport to the other camp, she probably would have been killed. So she really gave her life for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: She, she's-- and you know what? You found at a time like that that mothers and fathers would not have done things like that because when it-- during a war you become somehow or another so selfish. You only think of yourself. You only think of your own survival. And everybody was so surprised when they called Reva Mandelbaum that my sister walked out, and everybody knew her name wasn't Reva, you know? They knew she was Hannah, not Reva, but nobody said a word. Did the Germans know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody knew. Nobody knew. She walked out. When they called Reva Mandelbaum, she walked out, and everybody in the camp knew, of course, who I was. And so you didn't find too much-- too many things like that, because you became very, very selfish, very self-centered, because when you starve of hunger, when you have nothing to look forward to, you become like this. You know, you only think of yourself, and this is what we found.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now the Russians are there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians came in. They opened the gates. The gates were open and you became like a free bird but nowhere to go. No country, no people that you could talk to. The Germans had disappeared. Some of the Germans-- some of the Germans even dressed in civilian clothes because they wanted to mingle among the inmates of the camp because they knew if the Russians would see the Germans, they would be captured right away. And they did capture a lot. They captured the leader of the camp and quite a lot of the women of the camp. The Gestap-- the kapos, you know, the people that work for the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Jewish people, they just did not know where to go. You know, it's-- where do you go? It's not your country, you've been cooped up for five years in camps. What do you do? Well, I think everybody had the same thought that we did. We sort of all knew that when we survived, when we will survive, that we would have the central point where we would go back, and it was Demblin, the hometown where my grandparents were. And this is where we had decided to go. And how do you go? There's n-- there's no cars. So what we used to do was walk. We'd walk the whole time. I had absolutely no warm clothing. My feet were completely with wounds from the cold. My feet were wrapped with rags. My sister had wooden, wooden shoes that she wore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now mind you, this was January 17, 1945, in the bitter of winter. In Polish-- Poland, the winters are just as harsh, even harsher than Minnesota winters. And we walked. We decided to walk. But at that point we still were very much afraid of the Polish people, and we walked pretending that we were not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we walked from farm to farm slowly. We would-- night-- at night, we would find a barn somewhere, and we would sleep overnight. And we would beg for food. We became beggars.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was only the one-- because being that I was the youngest and the smallest, my sisters always pushed me to go. You know, she always thought, well, they would have more pity on a little girl than maybe on an older person, so they would give me a piece of bread here and a piece of bread there. And we would travel. We would go back from Czestochowa all the way back to Demblin by foot in January.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. It was-- it was quite a ways because it took us almost a whole day by train. I-- off-hand, I don't know how many kilometers it was. But it was a day and a half by train, so it had to be quite a ways. I think it took us a few weeks to reach Demblin. And, of course, without clothing, without food, we used to beg. Like I said, I begged for food. I would go in. And at that time the farmers themselves did not have very much either because they too-- it was a war for them too. But some of them were very kind, and they would give us bread. And some of them were not so kind. Some of them would yell at us, dirty Jew, [NON-ENGLISH]. Dirty Jew, get out. And some of them were kind. Some of them were kind. As a matter of fact, I remember once coming into a Polish home where they were giving us food, and they would give us shelter. And they told us we could bathe, and it was-- they were very, very warm. But one day this was still going on. Going back, as we were leaving the camp-- I left this out. As we were leaving the camp, the Russians were still bombing the city because, you know, when you were liberated you became like an animal and the gates were open and you just ran in different directions. Well, the three-- my sister Anne and my sister Eda and I stuck together, and as we were walking out of the camp, the Russians were still bombing the camps and they were bombing the city. And a bomb fell right in front of us. One guy was killed, another girl was wounded, terribly wounded. My sister was wounded on her head, but it was a flesh wound, but blood was starting coming out of-- and we could see the blood in the snow. And we didn't know-- we thought for sure she has really been hit badly, but fortunately for her, it was just a scratch type. It wasn't a deep cut. But it was bad enough where blood was coming out and would see the blood in the snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: We finally were able-- and my sister Eda, as she was hearing the bombs-- a bomb makes a certain sound when it's ready to fall. And as my sister Eda heard that sound, she grabbed me so hard, and she pulled me into the-- to the-- what are they called? Where the soldiers were laying? What are those called? Where the soldiers hide? A trench?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trench. Trenches. I couldn't think of the word. Trenches.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister Eda pulled me so hard that I fell into the trench, and I was saved because had I walked, the bomb would have hit me too. And finally after the bombs had calm-- you know, quieted down and there were no bombs falling anymore, then we decided to walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we finally found a barn to hide in, and when we got into the barn, we were lucky. In that house there were Russian, Russian soldiers, and they told us to come into the house. And this girl that was so terribly wounded, her whole stomach was just open. It was so horrible. You could see all the intestines inside. And she begged us. She begged us, she says, please, please kill me. She says, please, I don't want to live. And, of course, she died right in front of us because she was just-- her whole stomach was torn apart. And then we got into the-- to the home where the Russians were, the soldiers, and they were able to bandage my sister's head. They cut off some of the hair and they were able to bandage her hair. It was mainly a flesh wound, but it was pretty deep. Thank God for them that they were able to clean her up. And then they gave us some food. And, and we were on our way. We stayed there overnight, and then we decided to keep going. And then we kept walking and walking and walking till finally we reached Demblin. And Demblin was the city where we had the camp for two years. That's the same place. And, of course, when we got there January 1945, there was nobody there. Nobody had survived. And we were there for a little bit, for a little while. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hope to see there when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were hoping that we would find-- our father we knew was dead because our father had been killed right away. We were hoping that maybe my mother or my sister, aunts and uncles, somebody could have survived. We were hoping that we would find somebody because we sort of had a central point that we said if anybody survives, we're going back to there. We knew that there was nothing in Warsaw because Warsaw was destroyed during the uprising of April 1943 when they had the uprising. So we knew Warsaw was not left anymore to go back to. And Demblin was a small town. We thought we will go back there thinking that maybe my aunts and uncles had survived. But when we came there, there was nothing there, and the Polish people were not very nice. They just were not nice. They did not treat us very nicely. We found a bombed-out house without a roof and whoever was there, the people that came, you know, gathered just like we did, we stayed in that house. It was the bitter cold, bitter winter. No food. The Russian even had less food than the Germans. They had nothing, the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to go to the garbage looking for food, and most of the time I would find like potato peelings. And I would bring it home to this house where we stayed-- this bombed-out house-- and we would boil it in water and eat it. To today, I cannot stand a potato with peelings because it still reminds me of the potato peelings I ate, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed there awhile in this city, Demblin. We wanted to wait and see if somebody would come, maybe somebody, somebody by a miracle would survive. And, of course, nobody had. Nobody came back. Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there till I think March, because I think we stayed a few months. We waited there. This was January. We stayed until it got warmer, I think April or March. And we had heard that the city of Lublin, which is not far from Demblin, which is about maybe 50 kilometer, it was sort of the gathering point of the Jewish people. A lot of people were coming back to that, and they were organizing. They at that point already had an organization. People were organizing, trying to put-- it was an army barracks and where they had army cots and that's where they organized all the people to come. And when you came there, you put-- they put names on a certain list of the survivors who survived. So, in other words, whoever came back, they could see a name of people. And, of course, you looked and you looked and you couldn't-- you couldn't find your own name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Yep.
